name: Rational Numbers — Fractions, Decimals and Between
whatIsIt: A RATIONAL number is any number you can write as a fraction p/q (with q not zero) — this includes whole numbers, negatives, fractions and many decimals. As decimals they either STOP (terminating: 0.25) or REPEAT forever in a pattern (0.333…). Between any two rational numbers there's always another — they are densely packed on the number line.
whyNeeded: Most everyday numbers — prices, measurements, shares — are rational. Understanding that fractions and decimals are the same family, and that you can always find a number between two others, is key to the real number line and algebra.

vocabulary.meaning: A number that can be written as p/q with q ≠ 0.
A decimal that repeats forever, like 0.666…

story.title: The Number In Between
story.text: Miss Iyer asks Aryan to name a number between 1/2 and 3/4. Aryan turns them into decimals: 0.5 and 0.75. 'Anything in between works — like 0.6!' he says. 'And as a fraction?' He picks 5/8 = 0.625. 'There's always room for more,' Miss Iyer smiles — 'rationals are dense.'
story.answerInStory: 0.6 (or 5/8) — between 0.5 and 0.75; there are infinitely many.

visual.caption: Fractions are numbers on the line. Between any two there's always another — infinitely many.

teachingGallery.title: Fractions, decimals, and between
teachingGallery.note: Rationals stop or repeat; you can always fit one in between.

standardMethod.summary: Write a number as p/q to test if it's rational; convert to decimal to see terminating vs repeating; average two numbers to find one between.
steps: Rational check: can it be written as a fraction p/q (q ≠ 0)? Whole numbers and terminating/repeating decimals all can.
Decimal type: divide top by bottom — it either ends (terminating) or repeats a pattern.
To find a number BETWEEN two: take their average (add them, divide by 2), or compare as decimals.
There are infinitely many rationals between any two — they are dense.

workedExamples.problem: Is 0.75 rational? Write it as a fraction.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking a repeating decimal like 0.333… is not a real/rational number.
commonMistakes.fix: Repeating decimals ARE rational — 0.333… = 1/3. Only never-ending, non-repeating decimals are irrational.

commonMistakes.mistake: Believing there is no number between two close fractions like 1/2 and 3/4.
commonMistakes.fix: There's always one — average them. Between any two rationals lie infinitely many more.

hintLadder: Can it be written as p/q?

challenge.q: Meena says there's no number between 1/2 and 3/4. What's the fix?

questions.q: A rational number can always be written as…

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up that a price like ₹2.50 is a rational number by writing it as a fraction (5/2), and find a number that sits between 1/2 and 3/4 by averaging — explaining that between any two numbers there's always another.

revisionCard.summary: Rational = can be written p/q (q ≠ 0): whole numbers, negatives, fractions, terminating (0.25) and repeating (0.333…) decimals. To find a number between two, take their average. Rationals are dense — infinitely many between any two.

rememberIt.hook: Rational = can be written as a fraction p/q (q not 0)
rememberIt.unpack: Includes whole numbers, decimals that end or repeat, and fractions. To compare, use a common denominator or convert to decimals.

studentQuestions.a: Yes - -3/4 is still p/q. Any fraction of integers with a non-zero bottom is rational.
Make the bottoms the same (15): 2/3 = 10/15, 3/5 = 9/15. So 2/3 is bigger.
